data.body:
## Decision record — Revision 2 (2026-06-17, post 2nd Opus review)

The 2nd review found R1.1+R1.2 mutually contradictory: `public_state(state)` only
receives the reduced dict, which carries `created_at` but NO last-event ts and NO
snapshot ts — so the `max(created_at, last-event-ts, snapshot-ts)` formula is not
computable there. Reviewer offered two clean mechanisms; we pick (a) — compute during
replay + strip at the single compaction serialization site — because it gives ALL
readers (lib/CLI/MCP/`public_state`) the field from ONE derivation point and touches
event bytes at exactly ONE place guarded by a byte-parity test, vs option (b) which
changes the `public_state` signature and every call site and would force `reduce_ticket`
to return side-channel timestamps to many callers (incl. the reconciler).

**R2.1 — SUPERSEDES R1.1: derive `updated_at` during replay, strip it at compaction.**
- In the reduce/replay path (`reducer/_api.py reduce_ticket` / `_processors.replay`),
  maintain a running max over event timestamps and set `state["updated_at"]` after
  replay. Seed = `created_at`; every replayed event's `timestamp` folds in. The field
  IS on the reduced dict (so all readers + `public_state` get it for free).
- `compact.py` is the ONLY path that serializes the reduced dict into event bytes
  (`compiled_state`, `compact.py:93,124`). It MUST `pop("updated_at")` from the dict
  BEFORE serializing `compiled_state`. (No other reduced-state -> event-bytes path
  exists; the reconciler writes from Jira->events, not from reduced state.)
- AC: SNAPSHOT `compiled_state` contains no `updated_at` key; canonical event bytes are
  byte-identical with and without this change (existing canonical-bytes parity test holds).

**R2.2 — SUPERSEDES R1.2: re-seed the max from the SNAPSHOT after the replay-skip.**
`replay_events` skips all pre-SNAPSHOT events (`_processors.py:430`) and
`process_snapshot` (`_processors.py:367`) restores `compiled_state` (which no longer
carries `updated_at` per R2.1). So `process_snapshot` MUST seed the running max from the
SNAPSHOT event's own `compacted_at`/`timestamp` (it lives in the SNAPSHOT event's outer
`data`, which replay otherwise discards). Final value:
`updated_at = max(created_at, latest post-snapshot event ts, snapshot compacted_at)`.
For a freshly-compacted ticket with no later events this correctly equals `compacted_at`
(>= created_at). AC: add a latest-event-timestamp accumulator to the replay loop (the
only place that sees post-snapshot event timestamps) and assert a compacted-then-untouched
ticket reports `updated_at == compacted_at`.

**R2.3 — clarify R1.3 scope (nit from review):** the None-last `(is_none, value)` tuple
sort key applies to EVERY new sort key, including `created`/`updated` — the new code must
not copy the pre-existing `key=... or 0` pattern at `reads.py:382` (correct for session
logs, wrong as a general sort technique).

Net effect on the epic's headline claims: still no SCHEMA_VERSION bump and no new event
TYPE; event BYTES are provably unchanged (the only serialization touch is a field-strip
guarded by a byte-parity AC). I3-clean and Jira-safe stand.
